_doc: Ordered list of Circuit Tutor tasks. Each task has: id, type (measure|problem|scenario|exploration), topicId, topicName, difficulty, data. Measure tasks pre-load a scaffold circuit with locked component ids. Edit this file to add/remove/reorder tasks.

data.brief: Complete the circuit so the ammeter reads the current through the resistor. Then type the reading below.
data.hint: The ammeter must be in series with R1. Form one complete loop.
data.explanation: I = V / R = 6 V / 3 Ω = 2 A.

data.brief: Connect the cell and voltmeter so V1 reads the p.d. across the bulb. Then type the reading.
data.hint: A voltmeter goes in parallel across the component you want to measure.
data.explanation: With only one bulb in the loop the full supply p.d. sits across it: 6 V.

data.brief: Finish the series loop of two resistors (2 Ω and 4 Ω) and an ammeter. What current does A1 read?
data.hint: In series, add the resistances. Then use I = V / R_total.
data.explanation: R_total = 2 + 4 = 6 Ω. I = 6 / 6 = 1 A. In series the current is the same everywhere.

data.brief: Wire the two resistors in parallel across the cell, with the ammeter measuring the TOTAL current from the cell. Enter A1's reading.
data.hint: The supply p.d. sits across each branch. Total I = I₁ + I₂.

data.question: A 6 V cell is connected in a series loop with a 2 Ω resistor. What is the current in the circuit?
workingSteps: Apply Ohm's law: V = I × R, so I = V / R
data.hint: What equation links voltage, current and resistance?

data.question: A 12 V cell is connected in a series loop with a 4 Ω resistor. What is the current in the circuit?
workingSteps: Apply Ohm's law: V = I × R, so I = V / R
data.hint: What equation links voltage, current and resistance?

data.question: A 9 V cell is connected in a series loop with a 15 Ω resistor. What is the current in the circuit?
workingSteps: Apply Ohm's law: V = I × R, so I = V / R
data.hint: What equation links voltage, current and resistance?

data.question: A 230 V cell is connected in a series loop with a 46 Ω resistor. What is the current in the circuit?
workingSteps: Apply Ohm's law: V = I × R, so I = V / R
data.hint: What equation links voltage, current and resistance?

data.question: A 6 V battery is connected in series with a 2 Ω resistor and a 4 Ω resistor. What is the p.d. across the 2 Ω resistor?
data.hint: In a series circuit the current is the same everywhere — find it first.

data.question: A 12 V battery is connected in series with a 3 Ω resistor and a 9 Ω resistor. What is the p.d. across the 3 Ω resistor?
data.hint: In a series circuit the current is the same everywhere — find it first.

data.question: A 9 V battery is connected in series with a 5 Ω resistor and a 10 Ω resistor. What is the p.d. across the 5 Ω resistor?
data.hint: In a series circuit the current is the same everywhere — find it first.

data.question: A 6 V cell is connected across two parallel branches: one with a 2 Ω resistor, the other with a 3 Ω resistor. What is the total current drawn from the cell?
workingSteps: In parallel, the p.d. across each branch equals the supply p.d.
data.hint: What stays the same across each branch of a parallel circuit?

data.question: A 12 V cell is connected across two parallel branches: one with a 4 Ω resistor, the other with a 6 Ω resistor. What is the total current drawn from the cell?
workingSteps: In parallel, the p.d. across each branch equals the supply p.d.
data.hint: What stays the same across each branch of a parallel circuit?

data.question: A 9 V cell is connected across two parallel branches: one with a 10 Ω resistor, the other with a 15 Ω resistor. What is the total current drawn from the cell?
workingSteps: In parallel, the p.d. across each branch equals the supply p.d.
data.hint: What stays the same across each branch of a parallel circuit?

data.narrative: You want to measure the current through the bulb AND the potential difference across it at the same time.
data.challenge: Place the ammeter and the voltmeter correctly on the circuit.

data.narrative: Two bulbs in series with a cell. When you close the switch neither bulb lights up.
data.challenge: Identify the most likely fault and test it in the simulator.

guidedQuestions: Build two identical bulbs in series. Then rebuild them in parallel. Which is brighter, and why?
What happens to the brightness of bulb A when you unscrew bulb B — in series, and in parallel?
discoveryGoals: Parallel branches share the supply p.d.; series branches share it.
Removing a bulb in series breaks the whole loop; in parallel, only that branch is affected.

data.brief: Build a simple series circuit with the cell, bulb and ammeter, then read the current on A1.
data.hint: In a series circuit the ammeter sits in the single loop. Use I = V / R.
data.explanation: One loop: I = V / R = 6 V / 6 Ω = 1 A. In series the current is the same everywhere.

data.brief: Wire the two bulbs in parallel across the 9 V cell and use V1 to measure the p.d. across bulb L1.
data.hint: The voltmeter goes in parallel across L1. Each parallel branch gets the full supply p.d.
data.explanation: Each branch sits directly across the cell, so V across L1 = supply = 9 V.

data.question: In a series circuit a resistor has 6 V across it and a current of 2 A flowing through it. What is its resistance?
workingSteps: In a series circuit the ammeter reading is the current through every component.
Apply Ohm's law: R = V / I
data.hint: Rearrange V = I × R to make R the subject.

data.question: A branch in a parallel circuit has 12 V across it and a current of 3 A flowing through it. What is the branch resistance?
workingSteps: Each branch in parallel gets the full supply p.d., so V across the branch = 12 V.
Apply Ohm's law: R = V / I
data.hint: Ohm's law relates the branch current and the branch p.d. Rearrange for R.

data.narrative: You have a 12 V cell, a resistor R1, an ammeter A1 and a voltmeter V1. Build a series circuit that lets you measure both the current through R1 and the p.d. across R1.
data.challenge: Verify that R1 is 4 Ω using A1 and V1 with R = V / I.

data.narrative: Two resistors are connected in parallel across a 12 V cell: R1 (the branch you care about) and R2 (a second branch). A1 should read the current through R1 only, and V1 should read the p.d. across R1.
data.challenge: Verify that R1 is 6 Ω using the branch readings on A1 and V1 with R = V / I.

data.concept: Bulb brightness in a series circuit
guidedQuestions: Build a single bulb in a loop with a cell. Now add a second identical bulb in series. Does each bulb look brighter or dimmer than before?
What happens to the ammeter reading when you add the second bulb in series?
If adding a bulb adds resistance, what does Ohm's law predict about the current?
The current is the same through every component in a series loop — why does that make the bulbs equally dim?
discoveryGoals: Current is the same everywhere in a series circuit.
Adding series components increases total resistance, so current drops.
Lower current means bulbs in series are dimmer than a single bulb alone.

data.concept: Bulb brightness in a parallel circuit
guidedQuestions: Build one bulb across a cell. Now add a second identical bulb in parallel with the first. Does the first bulb change brightness?
What does a voltmeter across each branch read, compared to the supply?
Use an ammeter in each branch and an ammeter in the main loop — how do the branch currents relate to the total current?
Why can bulbs in parallel stay just as bright, even when you add more of them?
discoveryGoals: Each parallel branch gets the full supply p.d.
Branch currents add together to make the total current.
Because p.d. across each branch is unchanged, bulbs in parallel stay bright.

data.concept: Real ammeters and voltmeters are not perfect
guidedQuestions: An ideal ammeter has zero resistance. What would happen to the current in a simple loop if the ammeter actually had a small resistance of, say, 1 Ω?
An ideal voltmeter has infinite resistance. If a real voltmeter had a finite resistance, how could it draw a small current through itself when connected across a component?
Place a voltmeter across a component in your circuit. Could its presence slightly change the p.d. it is trying to measure? Why or why not?
Overall: in what way might using a real meter perturb the very quantity you are trying to measure?
discoveryGoals: A real ammeter's non-zero resistance slightly reduces the current it is measuring.
A real voltmeter's non-infinite resistance draws a small current, which can slightly lower the p.d. across the component.
Measuring instruments can disturb the circuit — good measurements rely on meters being close to ideal.
